text: I ordered my meal and the food was wonderful.
Now someone sits next to me. What should I say?
Have you ever been to this cafe before?
Have you tried the local coffee?
I have lived here for two years.
I have been here since Monday.
Hi, is this seat free? I love this coffee shop. Have you been here before?
Yes, many times! I'm Daniel, by the way. Are you new in the city?
Nice to meet you, Daniel. I'm Tuca. I have been here for just two weeks. Have you tried the coffee from the south?
I have, actually. It's my favorite, so we already have good coffee in common. What has brought you to the city?
I run a coffee farm in Brazil, so I have come to learn about the local culture. Have you ever traveled to South America?
Not yet, but it's on my list! It has been great chatting with you. We should do this exchange again next week.
We have a lot in common!
It has been great chatting with you.

Hey Tuca, it's Mateo. Tomorrow you are meeting people at that cafe for the language exchange, so here are a few tips for good small talk. First, relax and start with a simple icebreaker, like a comment about the coffee or the weather. These are safe topics with strangers. Then, ask an experience question with the present perfect: have you been here before, or have you ever tried the local coffee? People love to share their experiences. When someone answers, find something you have in common and ask a follow-up question to keep the chat going. If there is an awkward silence, do not worry; just bring up a new topic. Remember to be polite and ask about the other person too, not only about yourself. You are very outgoing, so I know you will get along with everyone. Have fun and tell me how it goes!

Hey, it's Tuca. I just got back from the language exchange at the cafe and it was wonderful. I was a little nervous at first, but I started with a simple icebreaker about the coffee, and it worked perfectly. I met a really outgoing guy named Daniel. We have a lot in common; he loves coffee as much as I do. We made small talk for almost an hour. I asked him lots of experience questions, like have you ever traveled to Brazil, and he asked me about my farm. There was no awkward silence at all, because we kept asking follow-up questions. Honestly, I have never felt so confident speaking English with a stranger. I think I am finally getting along with the language. Next Thursday I am going back, and this time I will bring up my trip to Mexico City. Talk soon!

Tuca arrives at the cafe for the language exchange and sits down next to a stranger. She breaks the ice with some small talk about the coffee. She asks him an experience question with the present perfect. They discover they both love coffee and have a lot in common. Before leaving, they agree to meet again next Thursday.
Have you ever been to a language exchange?
A simple question is a great icebreaker to start talking.
We both love coffee, so we have something in common.
I have lived in this city for two years.
I have been here since Monday.
If there is an awkward silence, bring up a new topic.
We have a lot in common.
It has been great chatting with you.
